title: Custard creams
subtitle: Classic British sandwich biscuit with custard powder in both biscuit and filling
excerpt: Two custard-powder shortbreads sandwiching a buttercream filling also flavoured with custard powder. The biscuits are pressed rather than rolled and cut, using a shaped mould or the flat of a fork to create the traditional pattern. The custard powder contributes a pale amber colour and a vanilla-custard flavour distinctly different from plain shortbread.
sourceNotes: The custard cream is one of Britain's most recognisable biscuits, introduced commercially in 1908 and consistently one of the best-selling biscuits in the UK. The flavour comes from custard powder (a cornflour-based product with vanilla flavouring) rather than actual egg custard. The biscuit is a variant of a vanilla shortbread with custard powder replacing part of the flour. The home-made version uses the same ingredients in similar proportions to the commercial biscuit: butter, flour, custard powder, and icing sugar for the biscuit, with a matching custard buttercream.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unfilled biscuits for up to 3 months. Fill after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assembled custard creams keep in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 150°C. Low temperature preserves the pale colour; the biscuits should be just set and not golden on top.

recipeTools.notes: two baking trays, lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: Custard powder (Birds custard powder in the UK) is a cornflour-based powder flavoured with vanilla and coloured pale yellow. In biscuit doughs it replaces some of the plain flour: the cornflour reduces gluten development and makes the biscuit more tender, while the vanilla flavouring gives the characteristic custard taste. Custard powder also contributes a pale amber colour to both the biscuit and the filling without requiring egg yolks.

content.text: The flavour of a custard cream comes from

used in both the biscuit dough and the filling. The dough should be just firm enough to roll without cracking; if it crumbles, add the milk a teaspoon at a time. Roll to an even 4 mm thickness; thicker biscuits need more baking time and tend to be soft in the centre. The filling needs to be stiff enough not to squeeze out when bitten.

attrs.prepNote: only if the dough is too dry to roll

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C conventional (150°C fan) and line two baking trays with baking paper. Beat the butter and icing sugar together until just combined. Sift in the flour, custard powder, and cornflour; add the vanilla. Bring together into a smooth dough. If it is too dry, add the milk a teaspoon at a time. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes.

Roll the dough to 4 mm on a lightly floured surface. Cut into 4 × 3 cm rectangles (or use a custard cream mould stamp). Prick each biscuit twice with a fork. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until set and barely coloured. Leave on the trays for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack to cool completely.

For the filling, beat the softened butter until pale. Sift in the icing sugar and custard powder and beat together. Add the vanilla. The filling should be stiff enough to hold its shape. Sandwich pairs of cooled biscuits with a teaspoon of filling each, pressing gently together. Leave to set for 10 minutes before serving.
